moveAnnotations.annotation: We begin with the Zukertort Opening, a flexible first move that controls the central square e5 and prepares to support either a kingside or central setup—we're not committed to the typical e4 pawn advance just yet. By developing our knight to its natural square, we gain tempo and keep Black guessing about our plans while avoiding the heavily analyzed positions of 1.e4 and 1.d4. This move embodies the modern hypermodern philosophy: we'll control the center from afar rather than occupy it immediately.

Black responds with the Pirc Invitation, establishing a flexible pawn structure and preparing to develop the bishop to f6 to contest central control—this move invites us to build a strong pawn center (typically with e4 and d4) so Black can counterattack it dynamically. The d6 pawn supports a future e5 advance and opens the diagonal for Black's light-squared bishop, which is a key feature of Pirc-type systems. Black is signaling that we should stake a claim in the center, but they'll have active piece play and counterplay to compensate for any space disadvantage.
